title: Vitamin C Deficiency Risk Assessment
description: Take our free Vitamin C deficiency quiz to assess your risk. Check for symptoms like bleeding gums, slow wound healing, and fatigue in this science-based screening.
scientificBasis: Based on clinical symptoms of hypovitaminosis C as documented in the American Journal of Clinical Nutrition and scurvy staging criteria. Early deficiency symptoms (fatigue, poor wound healing, bleeding gums) appear at serum levels below 23 µmol/L.
seoIntro:
Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) is one of the most essential nutrients your body needs for immune function, collagen synthesis, wound healing, and antioxidant protection. Despite being widely available in fruits and vegetables, vitamin C deficiency remains more common than many people realize — particularly among smokers, older adults, and those with limited dietary variety.

Early signs of vitamin C deficiency can be subtle and easy to dismiss: persistent fatigue, slow-healing cuts, frequent bruising, and bleeding gums. Left unchecked, more serious symptoms can develop, including joint pain, dry skin, weakened immunity, and in severe cases, scurvy.

This self-assessment is designed to help you evaluate whether your symptoms and lifestyle factors align with known indicators of vitamin C insufficiency. The questions are based on clinical screening criteria used in nutritional medicine and published research on hypovitaminosis C.

Please note: this quiz is an educational screening tool, not a medical diagnosis. If your results suggest elevated risk, we recommend consulting a healthcare provider and requesting a serum ascorbic acid blood test for confirmation.

questions.text: How often do you experience bleeding or swollen gums when brushing your teeth?
How would you describe your wound healing speed?

options.label: Wounds heal quickly with no issues
Noticeably slow — cuts take a long time to close
Very slow — wounds often reopen or get infected

questions.text: How often do you bruise easily or notice unexplained bruises?

options.label: Very frequently — I bruise from minor contact

questions.text: How would you rate your daily fruit and vegetable intake?

options.label: 5+ servings per day including citrus, peppers, or berries

questions.text: Do you smoke or are you regularly exposed to secondhand smoke?
How often do you experience unexplained fatigue or low energy?
Do you notice dry, rough, or bumpy skin (especially on upper arms or thighs)?

options.label: Persistent rough, dry, or splitting skin

questions.text: How often do you experience joint pain or swelling without a known injury?
Do you frequently get colds or infections?

options.label: Rarely — once a year or less
Very often — I feel like I'm always fighting something

questions.text: Do you experience nosebleeds without obvious cause?

results.title: Low Risk — Vitamin C Status Likely Adequate
results.description: Your symptoms do not suggest vitamin C deficiency. Maintain your current diet rich in fruits and vegetables to continue supporting healthy vitamin C levels.
recommendations: Continue eating vitamin C-rich foods like citrus fruits, bell peppers, and berries
No immediate action needed — maintain your current healthy habits

results.title: Mild Risk — Suboptimal Vitamin C Possible
results.description: Some symptoms are consistent with suboptimal vitamin C levels. Consider increasing citrus fruits, bell peppers, and leafy greens in your daily diet.
recommendations: Add more citrus fruits, berries, and bell peppers to your diet
Consider keeping a food diary for a week to track vitamin C intake
Ensure you're eating at least 5 servings of fruits and vegetables daily

results.title: Moderate Risk — Vitamin C Insufficiency Indicators Present
results.description: Multiple symptoms align with clinical signs of vitamin C insufficiency. A dietary review and possible serum ascorbic acid test are recommended.
recommendations: Consult a dietitian to review your vitamin C intake
Request a serum ascorbic acid blood test from your healthcare provider
Significantly increase vitamin C-rich foods in your diet
Consider a vitamin C supplement after consulting with a healthcare professional

results.title: High Risk — Possible Vitamin C Deficiency
results.description: Your symptom profile strongly suggests possible vitamin C deficiency. Consult a healthcare provider for blood testing and guided supplementation.
recommendations: See a healthcare provider as soon as possible
Request a serum ascorbic acid blood test
Discuss vitamin C supplementation with your doctor
Review your overall diet with a registered dietitian

faq.question: What are the most common symptoms of vitamin C deficiency?
faq.answer: The most common early symptoms include bleeding or swollen gums, easy bruising, slow wound healing, persistent fatigue, and dry or rough skin. Severe deficiency (scurvy) can cause joint pain, anemia, and tooth loss.

faq.question: How much vitamin C do I need daily?
faq.answer: The recommended daily allowance is 75 mg for women and 90 mg for men. Smokers need an additional 35 mg per day. Most people can meet this through diet alone by eating citrus fruits, bell peppers, strawberries, and broccoli.

faq.question: Can I take too much vitamin C?
faq.answer: The upper limit is 2,000 mg per day for adults. Excess vitamin C is excreted in urine, but very high doses can cause gastrointestinal issues like diarrhea, nausea, and stomach cramps. Megadoses may also increase kidney stone risk.

faq.question: Who is most at risk for vitamin C deficiency?
faq.answer: High-risk groups include smokers, people with limited fruit and vegetable intake, individuals with malabsorption conditions, and those with chronic illnesses. Older adults and people with alcohol use disorder are also at elevated risk.

sources: Carr, A.C. & Maggini, S. (2017). Vitamin C and Immune Function. Nutrients, 9(11), 1211.
Maxfield, L. & Crane, J.S. (2023). Vitamin C Deficiency. StatPearls Publishing.
National Institutes of Health. Office of Dietary Supplements — Vitamin C Fact Sheet.
